transcript.transcript: My name is Nancy Inaba, and I'm, um, part of the committee working on the Idaho Watercolor Society 50th anniversary. Uh, today's date is November the 18th, and I am with Connie Pepper to talk about her experience with Idaho Watercolor Society. This oral history is conducted with support of Idaho Historical Society as part of the America 250 in Idaho commemoration. The Oral History Project, of which this interview is a part, supports the camera commemorations mission to advance the ideals of the American Revolution, equality, liberty, and justice through meaningful community engagement and recognize, appreciate, and commemorate Idaho's journey in America's legacy. Connie Pepper. First, let me thank you so much for your time today. This interview will allow you to reflect on and share your memories and experiences about our 50th anniversary of AWS. Through these questions, I hope to gain insight into an interview outcome. Uh, and the. And the history of Idaho Idaho Watercolor Society. Um, I will begin with. Some general questions. Then we can get into more specific things during the interview. I may adjust the order to allow a more natural flow. Um, so before we jump into the questions, do you give permission to record this interview? And can I have you state and spell your first and last name? I certainly grant permission. My name is Connie Pepper. C o n n ie P e pper. Okay. All right. Well, so, Connie, can you tell me something about yourself and how long you've been a representative? Or the director regional director for your area? I joined IWS in about 2003, and within a couple of months was made a a representative along with two other ladies. They they pulled me in by saying, you know, we just need a little help and would you be part of it? So two other, two other people and I, um, were reps for a while. And of course, then they slept off and decided, oh, you know, I can't do this. I'm doing another job for AWS or, you know, anyway, I ended up being the sole rep for, uh, about 20 years. Really? Yes. So, uh, I'm assisting her and getting going and also doing some of the little jobs that we have for IWS. So that's incredible. So I know you've done an incredible job as a regional director. What kinds of things are you doing to keep the interest in art going. For myself or for for my members. For your members and for yourself? Okay. Um, for myself, uh, I paint almost every day. I love my sketchbooks. Um, I have a whole shelf full of those. And continuing to teach classes in how to do that. Uh, when I was rep, which I'm not right now, I did a number of things. My region is huge. Uh, we go all the way to Sun Valley up to, uh, Rupert Burley area, over to Hagerman. Um, Wendell, you know, it's just it's a big, big, big loop. So that was one of the challenges I had as the rep for AWS is, um, being able to stay in touch with people. And fortunately, we had email then, and, uh, some had it, some didn't. Um, and we when I first was rep, we would meet just a couple of times a year and we would have, uh, short little meetings where it wasn't much of a meeting, but mostly an art gathering. And they had bought an old school house, and they had turned it into an art center. Over in the Burley Rupert area. And, uh, we just had a great time painting in this old school house. Um, we'd meet down in the canyon, uh, along the river with different people. Um, especially Roy Mason. Um, and Joyce Deford, who was also a wonderful IWS painter, um, influenced me as well. But, I mean, we did a small painting and it was kind of surprised that it looked like, well, this maybe is attainable. Yeah. We had him come up to that Sun Valley group that I meet with. That painter paints outdoors. He did a marvelous job. So part of that, what happens with that group, too, is that they do invite instructors to come. And we've had a number of really good instructors. And so that brought the Boise model of bringing people in for an annual show to, you know, do it in their hometown where they don't have to travel. And so, um, they've been doing that. That same gal I was talking about, Linda. Um, she started that and and she's worked with a lot of people that come in and, and do small classes up there, which we've opened up to, uh, advertising via the web through AWS. And some people have come for that. I think that's a great opportunity. And I think that traveling the distance once in a while kind of bridges the gap. It makes the instead of a region. It makes it a state organization. Yes, yes. And and I think that's real helpful, especially having Hugh, who's a Boise, Idaho. representative, um, that was an eye opener for that group up there, because they'd always had somebody from California or, you know. Yes. Yeah. Good name artists and gal from Oregon. It was wonderful. But they weren't IWS members. And so to have a member come and do the quality class that he did was really helpful. Yeah. I think it's good to recognize how many incredible artists we have here. I think so too. Yeah. Um, how many we can learn from? Um, in fact, Beth has won the Art and Soul prize, I think two years in a row now. Is that right? Yeah, yeah. So they're starting to come, you know, competition even stiffer here. So. Yes, yes. Good. Well, um, do you have any outstanding stories or memories about your time with IWS? Oh, gosh. Um. I think I've kind of relayed most of them. I have come to some of them to the workshops, um, and left with with good feelings about the workshops for the most part. Um, you know, that's the connection that I have. And of course, all the friendships I've made here with people and, and, um, it was a real honor to be the rep for such a long time. And I just finally got out of the way and decided it was time for somebody else to add new value to it and give me more time to paint. You know, I think that's, um, wise and and you've been doing for a long time. But, you know, if we don't bring the young people up to take some roles or new people, then it stays only in a small group of people. Right. And and Michelle is, is fairly newly retired. She's been here about five years and, um, after retirement. And so she was just right for the job, which I think will be really great. Um, one thing that occurs to me, uh, that's been helpful in the linkage to the overall IWS is the traveling show. Um. When it comes, it hangs in the lobby at, at the Twin Falls Center for the Arts. And the viewing public is really receptive to that show. Um. I've heard that you have incredible numbers. Yeah. And great people coming through, um, to see the show and and that's really been helpful. Um, I can't say that, uh, um, doing the boxes and hanging the show, you know, I have so few members that it it's kind of fall into, uh, just a few of us that are are able because I have a lot of people who are not able. Um, but, uh, when you get past that part of it, hanging into putting it away. Um. Yeah, it's really been a real boon for us. And it does take a lot of volunteer people to make these things happen. It shows the old newsletters. And before the age of internet, it was all like letters and or telephone calls. It was even more laborious. Yes. And and the efficiency. See. Um, and also, um, I have to compliment, uh, Carol and the rest of the board. They came to Twin Falls to meet with me and see where we work and get my input. And that was really a valuable experience. And I think an eye opener for them as well. Um, I think that there have been some real leaps and bounds, and I really compliment Carol. She's been a great manager and the rest of the board, they all are such hard working people and and everybody steps up. I just the zoom meetings where I've been able to come to the meeting has certainly made me more connected. Um. Overall, IWS, um, that's been valuable. I don't always make it to those, um, I don't always have a lot of input, but, um. It you can hear the process. Yes. Hear the process and hear what the plans might be. And, um, can can better serve the group that I was working with to, uh, you know, make our group even better. You know, got a lot of inspiration from that, that that was a big leap, um, in, I think, connecting the regions to IWS. Yeah. I think sending um, it sounds like that ambassadorship of going to the places and meeting the people is an important piece, like Hugh was an ambassador from Southwest Region. Oh, without being called that? Yes, exactly. Yeah. Um, you know, this is an IWS person. And look at what's happening here. And and people still talk about his workshop and what a good time they had. And remember when we were down by by Trail Creek and this happened and you know, it it those are good memories and very, very good ambassador for our region. And people see his work online and know you know I know him. Yeah. I think, um, it's something perhaps we could look at doing more. Not formal, but maybe sponsoring that kind of thing where we can send an artist to a different region and vice versa. Um, so that there becomes more interaction with the different regions. So we're not isolated. And Carol tried that. I was supposed to to work at the annual meeting last year and I got Covid. So yes, that was so I, you know, put things together. I was ready to go and, you know, feeling good about it. And then Covid hit and thankfully Anne Sorenson jumped in and said, well, you know, I'll do it, you know, took a deep breath and, and I hear she did a marvelous job. So she did. I said to Anne, I can't believe that you did this in one day. She goes, well, she sent me all her notes. Yeah, yeah. I put together a PowerPoint. Thankfully, I had done that. You know, that's back to my old technology days. And, um, so she was able to use that to help guide her through. But, you know, she already told me some of the things that she added, because I said, you know, do whatever, do your own thing, you know, do whatever you want there. And, and so, um, I taught that class here, uh, at the Art center later, and I used some of the things that she had told me, I put those little activities in that she had told me about. And she was right. They were perfect for what we were doing. So anyway. Yeah, you've gone the whole gamut. So you're teaching art. How do you think that's affected you as an artist? Being a teacher of art? Oh, gosh. It it makes me, um, question my own process sometimes because I'm not I'm not practicing what I, what I'm preaching. And, um, yeah, I'm teaching a little sketchbook class, a holiday sketchbook class, and, um, I, I know that I taught the same sketchbook class that I had done, uh, or put together for Boise and realized when I taught that that it was too much PowerPoint and not enough doing, um, and people liked it, and they took good notes, and I had a bunch of doing in it. But, um, this one is just going to be almost all doing. And so we're, uh, working on drawing oh, drawing fundamental. And I don't know, I never know what my audience is going to be when I teach over here. Um, so we just start from the beginning and start from the basics, and, and then people take off and fly. Um, you never know what you can do until you do it. Because in theory, when you hear it or you see it, you think, oh, yeah, I could do that until you do. Or I couldn't do that. And if you make it then, then they figure it out. Um. Yeah, yeah, I think that it's very meaningful in, um, in people's lives that they're able to accomplish. I feel like that is a retired person, that even if I just learned this little bit, I've accomplished something I didn't do before. Mhm. Yeah. I jackrabbit around to what medium I'm using. Um, and part of that's due to the second Tuesday because it turned in. We first started by just getting together to paint which was fun and people would help each other. And I don't know how many times Roy had to answer the question, how do you mix for sage? You know, how do you mix that color for sage? And, um, then it evolved into, uh, a little more, uh, teaching. People would show what they were learning and how to do this. And so we've done jelly printing. We've done, uh, this last session, somebody uses gold leaf in their work. And, and I had had a a terrible time with that. So I said, would you show us what you do? And I was doing it all wrong, you know, just not going the step by step that she showed us. And so now I'm putting some of that in my work, and it's turning out much better than what I had done. And so that's. Exciting. So you're learning from each other. We are. And tapping into the things that that they know how to do. And that's been really helpful to that group. And really and they're not all IWS members, but they come and they they paint and they you know, they're not all watercolor painters. We tell them we don't care what medium. And we we have it's become largely a women's group. Uh, but we do have, uh, a couple of men who come and paint with us and, and that's, you know, really nice and, and so happy that they're still coming because it, it is, by and large, mostly women. It's always nice to have a little balance in the group. Yes, yes, that's exactly right. Well, I'm so grateful for the time that we spent together. Connie. Well. Me too. Thank you. You made it really easy. I appreciate it. I mean, I have my questions, but your your conversation just kind of flowed right through them, so. Well. You set me up pretty well with with what we would talk about. So that that helped. And I wrote some things down this morning. So I kind of had it in my head. Um, yeah. That was really helpful. Okay. I think what I well, this interview and the transcript will I think I can send it to you. I don't know how yet, but it will be available through the Idaho Historical Society if you ever wanted to share it with your family. Okay. I think it's kind of a nice story to keep in your family. Well, sure. And I'd like to see it myself just to say, oh, I wish I had said, You know, I wrote that down. Why didn't I tell her that? And I know I will go back, but. I can see when I do look at the transcript, I think, oh, I think I must have had a stroke. Oh, my language is all confused. Yeah, well, you've made it a real comfortable situation. And, um, you know, your your notes were helpful to give me some idea of what I needed to have in my head. Uh. I'm gleaning a lot about what's important about the association, about the society beyond art. That's the obvious thing. But there's so much more. Mhm. I agree with you. Relationships for me. Um, yeah. Yeah. It gets me out of the house and out of my studio. You know, it's just. And then the. And then you get to learn new things. Oh what a bonus. Yeah, exactly. Yeah. Well, you have a great holiday. I'm going to stop the recording. Okay. Thanks so much. Okay.

lines.line: # Introduction and Purpose of Interview

> Nancy Inaba introduces herself and sets the stage for the oral history interview, explaining its connection to the Idaho Watercolor Society's 50th anniversary and the America 250 in Idaho commemoration. She outlines the goals of the project, which include advancing the ideals of the American Revolution and commemorating Idaho's journey in America's legacy. Nancy also requests permission to record the interview and asks Connie Pepper to state and spell her name, establishing the formal beginning of the conversation.

metadata.title: Introduction and Purpose of Interview
metadata.synopsis: Nancy Inaba introduces herself and sets the stage for the oral history interview, explaining its connection to the Idaho Watercolor Society's 50th anniversary and the America 250 in Idaho commemoration. She outlines the goals of the project, which include advancing the ideals of the American Revolution and commemorating Idaho's journey in America's legacy. Nancy also requests permission to record the interview and asks Connie Pepper to state and spell her name, establishing the formal beginning of the conversation.

lines.line: # Connie Pepper’s Role and History with AWS

> Connie Pepper shares her history with the Idaho Watercolor Society, describing how she quickly became a regional representative after joining in 2003. She recounts how she ended up serving as the sole representative for about 20 years due to others stepping down, and discusses her recent transition to mentoring a new rep. Connie reflects on her long-term commitment and the responsibilities she has carried for the organization.

metadata.title: Connie Pepper’s Role and History with AWS
metadata.synopsis: Connie Pepper shares her history with the Idaho Watercolor Society, describing how she quickly became a regional representative after joining in 2003. She recounts how she ended up serving as the sole representative for about 20 years due to others stepping down, and discusses her recent transition to mentoring a new rep. Connie reflects on her long-term commitment and the responsibilities she has carried for the organization.

lines.line: # Activities to Foster Art Interest and Community

> Connie describes her personal dedication to painting and sketchbooks, as well as her efforts to teach classes and organize art gatherings for AWS members. She details the challenges of staying connected across a large region and the creative solutions she implemented, such as meeting in unique locations and inviting non-members to participate in hopes of growing the group. Connie highlights the importance of informal mentorship and the influence of fellow artists in fostering a vibrant art community.

metadata.title: Activities to Foster Art Interest and Community
metadata.synopsis: Connie describes her personal dedication to painting and sketchbooks, as well as her efforts to teach classes and organize art gatherings for AWS members. She details the challenges of staying connected across a large region and the creative solutions she implemented, such as meeting in unique locations and inviting non-members to participate in hopes of growing the group. Connie highlights the importance of informal mentorship and the influence of fellow artists in fostering a vibrant art community.

lines.line: # Membership Changes and Challenges

> The conversation shifts to the composition of AWS membership, noting that it is primarily made up of retired and older painters. Connie discusses the difficulties in attracting younger or new members due to work and family commitments, despite efforts to promote AWS through local organizations. She emphasizes the consistency of the group size and the ongoing challenge of maintaining and expanding membership.

metadata.title: Membership Changes and Challenges
metadata.synopsis: The conversation shifts to the composition of AWS membership, noting that it is primarily made up of retired and older painters. Connie discusses the difficulties in attracting younger or new members due to work and family commitments, despite efforts to promote AWS through local organizations. She emphasizes the consistency of the group size and the ongoing challenge of maintaining and expanding membership.

lines.line: # Workshops, Travel, and Costs

> Connie talks about her participation in workshops, the challenges of traveling long distances for week-long events, and the financial barriers faced by members. She shares experiences of members attending workshops in Stanley and presenting their learnings to the group, and reflects on her own prioritization of art education after early retirement. The discussion highlights the importance of workshops for artistic growth and the practical limitations that affect participation.

metadata.title: Workshops, Travel, and Costs

She shares experiences of members attending workshops in Stanley and presenting their learnings to the group, and reflects on her own prioritization of art education after early retirement. The discussion highlights the importance of workshops for artistic growth and the practical limitations that affect participation.

lines.line: # Connie’s Background and Move to Idaho

> Connie recounts her move to Idaho from North Dakota, describing family vacations that led to settling in Jerome in 1978. She shares memories of renting a house, starting a family, and gradually establishing roots in the area. The narrative provides insight into her personal journey and the leap of faith involved in relocating to Idaho.

metadata.title: Connie’s Background and Move to Idaho
metadata.synopsis: Connie recounts her move to Idaho from North Dakota, describing family vacations that led to settling in Jerome in 1978. She shares memories of renting a house, starting a family, and gradually establishing roots in the area. The narrative provides insight into her personal journey and the leap of faith involved in relocating to Idaho.

lines.line: # Building a Home and Developing as an Artist

> Connie discusses building a home on the Snake River canyon and the encouragement she received from her architect to include a dedicated art studio. She describes her return to college to learn drawing, her experiences in art classes, and her husband’s later involvement in watercolor painting. The chapter highlights the development of a shared artistic passion within her family and the creation of personal creative spaces.

metadata.title: Building a Home and Developing as an Artist
metadata.synopsis: Connie discusses building a home on the Snake River canyon and the encouragement she received from her architect to include a dedicated art studio. She describes her return to college to learn drawing, her experiences in art classes, and her husband’s later involvement in watercolor painting. The chapter highlights the development of a shared artistic passion within her family and the creation of personal creative spaces.

lines.line: # Art Education and Family Engagement

> Connie reflects on her decision to return to school for drawing, the importance of foundational skills, and her ongoing engagement with college art classes. She shares stories about fostering creativity in her grandchildren, encouraging them to use quality art supplies, and supporting their artistic achievements despite challenges like colorblindness. The chapter emphasizes the role of art as a common language and source of joy within her family.

metadata.title: Art Education and Family Engagement
metadata.synopsis: Connie reflects on her decision to return to school for drawing, the importance of foundational skills, and her ongoing engagement with college art classes. She shares stories about fostering creativity in her grandchildren, encouraging them to use quality art supplies, and supporting their artistic achievements despite challenges like colorblindness. The chapter emphasizes the role of art as a common language and source of joy within her family.

lines.line: # Early Art Experiences and Self-Education

> Connie describes her beginnings in watercolor painting after retirement, her self-directed learning through books and workshops, and the influence of mentors. She shares anecdotes about early successes and the process of developing artistic skills, highlighting the importance of persistence, curiosity, and capturing the spirit of a subject over technical perfection.

metadata.title: Early Art Experiences and Self-Education
metadata.synopsis: Connie describes her beginnings in watercolor painting after retirement, her self-directed learning through books and workshops, and the influence of mentors. She shares anecdotes about early successes and the process of developing artistic skills, highlighting the importance of persistence, curiosity, and capturing the spirit of a subject over technical perfection.

lines.line: # Geographical Challenges and Technology’s Role

> The conversation turns to the difficulties of connecting AWS members across a large geographical region, the limited frequency of in-person meetings, and the sense of separation from the larger Boise group. Connie discusses the positive impact of technology, such as Zoom meetings and an expanded web presence, in improving communication and engagement among members. She also addresses the challenge of demonstrating the value of AWS membership to those hesitant to participate in contests or shows.

metadata.title: Geographical Challenges and Technology’s Role

Connie discusses the positive impact of technology, such as Zoom meetings and an expanded web presence, in improving communication and engagement among members. She also addresses the challenge of demonstrating the value of AWS membership to those hesitant to participate in contests or shows.

lines.line: # Partnerships and Local Art Community

> Connie explains the development of the Twin Falls Center for the Arts, its fundraising efforts, and the partnership with the Magic Valley Arts Council. She describes the facilities available for artists, the benefits of having a consistent meeting space, and the positive impact on community engagement. The chapter highlights the importance of local partnerships in fostering art activities and attracting new members.

metadata.title: Partnerships and Local Art Community

She describes the facilities available for artists, the benefits of having a consistent meeting space, and the positive impact on community engagement. The chapter highlights the importance of local partnerships in fostering art activities and attracting new members.

lines.line: # Art and Soul Show and Recognition

> The discussion focuses on the Art and Soul show, its origins, growth, and significant prize offerings. Connie shares her experiences participating in the contest, receiving recognition, and selling artwork. She emphasizes the role of the show in increasing visibility for artists and the value of community support for the arts.

metadata.title: Art and Soul Show and Recognition
metadata.synopsis: The discussion focuses on the Art and Soul show, its origins, growth, and significant prize offerings. Connie shares her experiences participating in the contest, receiving recognition, and selling artwork. She emphasizes the role of the show in increasing visibility for artists and the value of community support for the arts.

lines.line: # Book Projects and Artistic Output

> Connie talks about her work on children's books, a cookbook, and other illustrated projects, many of which were inspired by family and community connections. She describes the process of creating these books, their ongoing popularity, and the satisfaction of seeing her work become family heirlooms and community treasures. The chapter illustrates the diverse ways Connie has contributed to the local art scene.

metadata.title: Book Projects and Artistic Output
metadata.synopsis: Connie talks about her work on children's books, a cookbook, and other illustrated projects, many of which were inspired by family and community connections. She describes the process of creating these books, their ongoing popularity, and the satisfaction of seeing her work become family heirlooms and community treasures. The chapter illustrates the diverse ways Connie has contributed to the local art scene.

lines.line: # Sun Valley Art Groups and Regional Connections

> Connie discusses the art groups in the Sun Valley area, including nature journaling and botanical garden classes, and her involvement with these communities. She shares stories of meeting new people, forming lasting friendships, and promoting AWS within these groups. The chapter highlights the importance of regional connections and the role of art in building relationships.

metadata.title: Sun Valley Art Groups and Regional Connections

She shares stories of meeting new people, forming lasting friendships, and promoting AWS within these groups. The chapter highlights the importance of regional connections and the role of art in building relationships.

lines.line: # Mentorship, Relationships, and Influence

> Connie reflects on the deep connections and friendships formed through art, mentioning influential mentors and fellow artists who have shaped her journey. She emphasizes the significance of relationships within the art world, the ongoing communication with peers, and the impact of these bonds on personal and artistic growth.

metadata.title: Mentorship, Relationships, and Influence
metadata.synopsis: Connie reflects on the deep connections and friendships formed through art, mentioning influential mentors and fellow artists who have shaped her journey. She emphasizes the significance of relationships within the art world, the ongoing communication with peers, and the impact of these bonds on personal and artistic growth.

lines.line: # Opportunities for Connection and Technology

> The conversation explores opportunities for building stronger bonds within AWS, including the creation of the Tuesday art group and the use of technology to bridge regional gaps. Connie discusses the benefits of critiques, classes, and sharing experiences, while acknowledging the limitations of remote interactions compared to in-person connections. The chapter underscores the evolving role of technology in fostering community.

metadata.title: Opportunities for Connection and Technology

Connie discusses the benefits of critiques, classes, and sharing experiences, while acknowledging the limitations of remote interactions compared to in-person connections. The chapter underscores the evolving role of technology in fostering community.

lines.line: # Regional Ambassadorship and Workshops

> The discussion turns to the idea of sending artists between regions to foster statewide interaction and connection. Connie shares experiences with workshops led by AWS members and the positive impact of such events on regional groups. The conversation highlights the potential for ambassadorship and collaborative learning to strengthen the organization.

metadata.title: Regional Ambassadorship and Workshops

Connie shares experiences with workshops led by AWS members and the positive impact of such events on regional groups. The conversation highlights the potential for ambassadorship and collaborative learning to strengthen the organization.

lines.line: # Teaching Art and Its Impact

> Connie reflects on her experiences teaching art, the challenges of aligning her own practice with her teaching, and the evolution of her methods. She discusses the importance of hands-on learning, adapting to the needs of her audience, and the satisfaction of helping others discover their artistic abilities. The chapter highlights the reciprocal relationship between teaching and personal artistic growth.

lines.line: # Second Tuesday Group and Collaborative Learning

> Connie describes the evolution of the Second Tuesday group from informal painting sessions to collaborative learning experiences. She shares examples of members teaching each other new techniques, experimenting with different mediums, and fostering a supportive environment. The group is characterized by diversity in participants and artistic approaches, emphasizing the value of shared learning and community.

lines.line: # Closing Reflections and Interview Wrap-Up

> The interview concludes with expressions of gratitude, reflections on the ease and flow of the conversation, and plans for sharing the transcript with Connie and her family. Both participants acknowledge the importance of relationships, learning, and community within the Idaho Watercolor Society, and wish each other well for the holidays.

> The discussion turns to the idea of sending artists between regions to foster statewide interaction and connection. Connie shares experiences with workshops led by AWS members and the positive impact of such events on regional groups. The conversation highlights the potential for ambassadorship and collaborative learning to strengthen the organization.
# Teaching Art and Its Impact

> Connie reflects on her experiences teaching art, the challenges of aligning her own practice with her teaching, and the evolution of her methods. She discusses the importance of hands-on learning, adapting to the needs of her audience, and the satisfaction of helping others discover their artistic abilities. The chapter highlights the reciprocal relationship between teaching and personal artistic growth.
# Second Tuesday Group and Collaborative Learning

> Connie describes the evolution of the Second Tuesday group from informal painting sessions to collaborative learning experiences. She shares examples of members teaching each other new techniques, experimenting with different mediums, and fostering a supportive environment. The group is characterized by diversity in participants and artistic approaches, emphasizing the value of shared learning and community.
# Closing Reflections and Interview Wrap-Up

> The interview concludes with expressions of gratitude, reflections on the ease and flow of the conversation, and plans for sharing the transcript with Connie and her family. Both participants acknowledge the importance of relationships, learning, and community within the Idaho Watercolor Society, and wish each other well for the holidays.

story.title: Connie Pepper 50th Anniversary interview
story.description: 20 year Regional Director from Twin Falls Area
